speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 16th, 1996. Survivor is Evy Woods. Last name at birth is Goldstein. Interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Albuquerque, in the state of New Mexico. Country is United States of America, and the language is in English. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm supposed to look at you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is April 16th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor Evy Woods. The interview is being conducted in Albuquerque, New Mexico, in the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Louise.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My complete name now? It's Evy Dorothy Woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to spell all three names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: E-V-E-L-Y-N. And then Evy is E-V-Y. Dorothy, D-O-R-O-T-H-Y. Woods, W-O-O-D-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evelyn Thea Goldstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell-- you spelled Evelyn already. You are now known as Evy, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Thea, is that your middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-H-E-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-L-D-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Evi, which is pronounced in German 'A-vee.' E-V-I- or Y. And in English, became 'Ev-ee' or 'E-vee.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have any significance at all, the nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- I didn't even know my name was Evelyn until we immigrated for the United States. Never knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite 58.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, Germany, at a Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you recall of Berlin as a youngster? I know you were very young, but what memories do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. The things I remember most is a lot of display windows-- store display windows. You know, just unusually beautifully displayed. And [LAUGHS] a sparkling sidewalk, and not to step on the lines when you'd walk, but I don't remember anything particular. I knew my house-- apartment house that we lived in downtown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? Do you remember the name and number of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The last-- the last address that we had was Marburger-- named after the city of Marburg-- Strasse zwolf. 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you recall? Can you describe at all this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a large, probably four-story apartment building, almost a square complex. And the downstairs housed the post office, West 30. And it still is West 30, and it still is the post office. And it had a courtyard in which the post cars, or wagons, or however you would describe them-- not the little Jeeps like here-- were parked in the evening, and then taken out again. And yellow mailboxes. I remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your room at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Room? My mother and father and I really shared a room, and it was divided by a heavy cloth curtain so that I had no window in that. I slept in a little child's bed, still. There. You keep children children much longer than you do here. And I remember that I would be put to sleep. There was a nightstand there, but it was pitch dark once the curtain was drawn. And then they slept in the other part. We were lucky we had our own bathroom, although eventually there was no hot water, or you could only have hot water at certain time. With a toilet, with a pulling chain, you know, like they had in the olden days, great, big places. And I remember tile-- but genuine tile, not makeshift or mock tile floor. Kitchen, I don't remember. Yeah, I remember eating in a kitchen, but I think it may have been a shared kitchen already by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to share with other people in this flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents own the apartment house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. They were more or less assigned to live there. The people who owned it were an elderly couple named Mr. and Mrs. Levent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were probably about 70. And so they did not have to do forced labor. They were able to stay home. They had owned that building for years, and through the help of a lawyer-- a German lawyer, not Jewish-- they were able to keep a good part of their proceeds, which, of course, no one knew about. So he was a trusted attorney who eventually helped us to some degree as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about your parents. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his name was Ernst, E-R-N-S-T, like Ernest, but Ernst, Morry, M-O-R-R-Y, Goldstein. Same as mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe he was at that point a wholesaler of cigars. And cigars were a desired item. Therefore, some of his German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clients still continued to buy from him, even though it was long forbidden to be in any kind of business. But of course, one has to eat. And he had to go far into parts of Germany by train during the week in order to sell to retail places.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you-- how would you describe your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've heard him described as having a Berlin mouth. Berlin mouth is similar to a New York mouth, which means you say what you think and you say it on the spot. You don't weigh the consequence of what you say. Outspoken, quick-witted, humorous, daring. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A strict disciplinarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of all-- anything you did with him or with anything? Can you recall anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except being disciplined. I do recall that, on one occasion-- you're familiar with the brand of cream called Nivea?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nivea is sold in America in every grocery store now-- N-I-V-E-A. But it's made up differently than the way they made it up. They make it into a very rich cream. My mother had hoarded, during tough times already a two-pound-- you could imagine a box about this tall-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tin. And I remember it was a royal blue tin with white writing on it. Had hoarded that as cream to use. And I remember one morning, I had nothing to do in my dark, curtained-off place in my pajamas with the little teddies on it. And I began to smear this stuff all over myself, and on my face and into my hair. And I remember I was pretending that I was a nurse, because my mother's younger sister was a nurse. And what this had to do, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can tell you when they came in to get me out of my bed, I simply slipped away because I had this on my pajamas, all the two pounds. The jar was licked clean. And of course, we had no hot water. How do you get this off?

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father whooped the living daylights out of my behind. They tried carefully to scrape some of this stuff together to put it back, but it was impossible. But-- and then, I only remember him saying goodbye to me when I had to leave them-- when the three of us had to be split up to go into hiding. And other than that, I really don't remember him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to go to the train station in Berlin, which is still in existence, I believe. It's Anhalter Bahnhof. And it was a main station, huge, with many, many tracks for cattle cars, for passenger, for express, this and that. And we stood outside, up on the sidewalk with steps going down. And he sort of knelt down by me and said things that I don't remember. I was to go with this, quote, 'nice lady.' And that was that. By that time of course, I was already used to living without him or without my mother just about all the time. That was in '43. June, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since my birthday is in June, I guess I must have been five. Or maybe a week or two or three before I was five.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see that you are wearing a watch and a-- a ring. Was-- did that belong to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, I wore these today because I thought he should be here in some form. And the watch is running, although, it's never been cleaned or repaired. I set it this morning, wound it up-- it's a manual wind, of course-- and it's running perfectly. And the ring has his initials on it for Ernst Goldstein. And his brother had one that was TG for Theodore Goldstein. I think their father had these made [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for their 16th birthdays or something. But he always wore it. And we pulled this off the finger of a stranger selling baby buggies in East Berlin in 1948. He instinctively knew something was peculiar. And he said, you're not here for baby buggies. And my mother and I shook our heads and said, no. And he had his hands behind his back to hide them, as if he knew. And my mother said, I'm here for that ring that you're wearing. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was absolutely the ring? You recognized the ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. He took it off. He said, it is not mine. I got it during the hard times in the early post-war years, '45, '46. And he readily gave it back because my mother could have called the Russian police, which at that point would have given us the ring. And she gave him money for it, which he did not want. But she felt-- she's always been a person who is very just about everything. Gave him money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how she found the watch also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The watch was kept by my grandparents' neighbors named Baus, B-A-U-S. And particularly, Mr. Baus had been helpful to us during the underground, in hiding, days by supplying us with very expensive, gold-tipped-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call those? Fountain pens. I mean, they were valuable. You know, they are pens that cost, even here, hundreds of dollars. That kind of quality. And he gave us that to help us, if we sold them, to turn into money for food or whatever. They kept this and six of-- of my mother's original silverware and a watch that belonged to my mother that their daughter, who had been her girlfriend, although she was not-- they were not Jewish people. It was unusual to have friends that were not Jewish, but these families were close friends since 1922. The friend decided she wanted to keep that watch. My mother felt bad about it, but imagine these people kept these things during times when they could have turned that into food. So we mustn't be too judgmental about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Americans call it 'Herd-ah,' 'Hoor-da,' I've heard all versions. But it's Herta, Herta. H-E-R-T-A, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Long, L-O-N-G. People think she's Chinese.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Berlin, she was teaching foreign languages at home from the time they-- that my parents were married. She taught Italian, English, and French at home. And after I was born, this was very helpful because she didn't have to work. But she was very modern, always wanted to work, and did work. And my father encouraged it and said things like, dishes, cleaning, you can hire out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your mother when you were a little girl in Berlin? What was she like? And what memories do you have of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also not much. I remember her bending over my bed, which means it must have been very early, like 1941. I remember standing in my little bed. This was before moving into that place, Marburger Strasse 12. This was at our previous apartment, which was [INAUDIBLE] Strasse 26, which was leveled by the bombs later. I only remember that sort of thing, and lots of discipline. That was how it was in those days. You spent most of the time disciplining and never give too much praise because it may go to their heads and so forth. It was a way of raising children. She was raised that way also. Not so much hitting as voice and threat and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Punishments of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not speaking to you for two or three days. And telling you to think about what went wrong. And of course, you're running through the universe without a clue of what just went wrong. But it was a common style. I've compared it with German friends since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other memories you have of your mom and what you might have done together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you-- are you speaking still of the time before we went underground?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The answer is yes. I once-- she-- I saw her frequently lying in her bed, doing this with a pair of cuticle scissors. And she left the bed, and I got the scissors, and I had two tasks to perform. One was, in Germany, we did not tuck sheets into-- under the mattress. We let them hang.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first thing I had to do was cut the sheet off in round scallops. Actually, upside down scallops. And the second thing was I cut off all my eyelashes and eyebrows. And this must have been '42, 1942. And when she saw me next, she said she looked at me and thought, what is wrong here? This child looks like a little pig's head. There's something funny with the eyes. She couldn't identify right away. Well, of course, then I got it because I could have gouged my eye out, you know? But I remember, maybe once or twice, eating at our little kitchen-like table. But not particularly anything much. I remember maybe my kindergarten a little better than I remember home because I spent six days a week there, you know? Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of them to some degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which side was that on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Regina Loszinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember her-- I remember first myself standing in a doorway of a hospital room, and a bed far across from me. And she was leaning on one elbow toward me. So here was the bed and I'm over here, across. And she's saying, well, come closer. Come closer. She had been diagnosed with a sudden-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if it was colon cancer or stomach cancer. I just know it was cancer. And that whole process was treated, in those days, with cobalt treatments. Cobalt treatments either helped you live by destroying your cancer, or it killed you faster because it destroyed everything around that organ as well. And she had had many cobalt treatments for this. And the whole process, from diagnosis to death, was six weeks. And this must have been during the very last week or two in 1941. And we went to visit. And, you know, children are sometimes instinctive about death or something like that. I would not come closer, even though she had made dresses for me and things like that. I couldn't do it. I remember seeing sort of sunken in, dark black eyes, with terrible rings around the eyes. And dark hair. She was both dark-eyed and dark-haired and a little bit olive complected like my mother is. And that's all I remember. She was terribly offended, I'm sure, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other recollections of any of the other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Two of them were already deceased. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandfather, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about great grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. People didn't live as long as they do today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school-- again, prior to the Nazi occupation, what school were you-- did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I was only in, from the time I was very little, in a private kindergarten for a time, as long as it was allowed, which was kind of a half-day thing where you took your sandwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a private kindergarten. It's not the concept of kindergarten as in school. It means, like, nursery school. And the second was then it was mandatory for children to go to their own after everything got segregated. I went to a Jewish kindergarten, which was toward the end of the block slightly at an angle from our address on the same street, in a house that was the third house behind the front house at the street. That was common with courtyards, another building, courtyard, another building. And that's where all the Jewish kids were going. There were many of these scattered throughout the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of this kindergarten at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible. I remember hearing-- we had to take a nap there every afternoon with some blankets on some little low cots. And I remember hearing church bells every day at the same time. I never, ever slept. I don't take naps now, and didn't then. And I would lie there for whatever. And it was dismal, and it was gray, and the windows were small, and it was awful. And what we were allowed as food there, the six days a week, was red boiled beets. You hear it. Just it was as it sounds. There was no soup. There was no way to prepare them. They were peeled and boiled, and that's what you ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what kind of a smell that is? Beets have a very definite identifying odor. And that's what we ate. And it was dismal there, and people were constantly missing. People were missing because the Gestapo would come and fish several children out, knowing that their parents would volunteer when they didn't find their children at the kindergarten when they came home. So our parents must have been in desperate, insane fear. And we just knew they were missing. They were gone. And it was fewer and fewer. And so it was also with the women who worked there, caring for us, who were also, of course, all Jewish people. So it was always dismal. And you waited until finally your mother or father came to get you, everything in a rush because there were only certain hours you were allowed out on the street. And in some cases, the child was left standing simply because the parents had been picked up at their forced labor place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, you know, this child couldn't make the connection until probably the next day. Some overseeing person luckily always stayed around long enough till we were all picked up or-- or accounted for in some way with an adult person. But you knew what was going on, but you didn't understand what was going on. And then the way your parents hugged you and kissed you to say hello when they picked you up-- and they took turns at that. One had to go get the food. Remember, there were no refrigerators. So every day, you had to hassle on that limited diet that you had, in limited stores in which you could obtain them, which were all sold out or they didn't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, the other parent worried about, is the-- my child still at the kindergarten when I get there? And you could feel that. You could feel that by the way they clung to you in a desperate kind of a sensation. You know, you can tell that, but you don't understand why. Did you have any friends that you can recall that disappeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, actually. There's a little boy I remember named Michael. Or in German, it was Mikhail. And he was always sort of looking out for me. He was, I think, a year older than I was. My mother cannot remember the family's last name. I have asked her. She usually is a wealth of data, but she couldn't do it. And one day that was the end of Michael. Michael's father was a doctor. And then, there was a little girl named Bela. You know, in Europe, that's either a boy or a girl. Suddenly, Bela was missing, and I remember Bela and parents coming to our place on a Sunday for coffee or something, when that was still possible. And those were the only two people that I can recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall how it affected you not seeing them the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Because it's all-- when you're someone growing up, it's all a natural-- you think this is how the world is. You don't-- I don't think you suffer the way your parents suffer. I know how I feel when my daughter's in some kind of distress, or any family member. And that's because I'm an adult, and I, therefore, have the ability to feel that stress. It must have been very hard not to transfer a lot of these things to me. But fortunately, for the European upbringing style, that already was out of the question. You wouldn't share that kind of data with your children, or any significant data, until it became necessary. They insisted then that I had to share certain data with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would describe my religious background as zip. That means I went to a Jewish school. I only knew Jewish people as long as they were still there. But it was as a cautionary thing that my grandfather-- my mother's father-- and my parents decided this could not perpetuate that. And then you were only doing it at home by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't be out at strange hours. [LAUGHS] What if we did get to go underground? Then it would be safest if I didn't know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who I was, what we were, if I had no comprehension. And so in 1941, they stopped doing-- I remember my mother mentions a Seder dinner that my grandfather said, suddenly, when the main part was done, clear all this off. Get rid of all this off the table. Get this out of here. I don't want to see it again. And of course, you have to realize my grandmother had just passed away with the cancer business. And the Nazi thing had zeroed in. Forced labor was in effect. And so, out you could not get. Where would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we now learn, any of those who did go out-- say, to Holland or France-- that was all just temporary. They ran from country to country. It was not possible. And without money, which none of us had. That had already-- we had been relieved of anything that would represent money or value.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to know more what you mean by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you outgoing? Were you shy? Were you rebellious? How would you-- if somebody asked you, what were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Best as I can tell. Later, it apparently turned out I was very outgoing, and my mother called me her chatter box, so that I was, at times, a danger to the circumstances that followed-- in the years that followed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She always had to stay within earshot to make sure, to call me away as I was about to play, say, telephone with a girl named Uta, a German girl, by the time we were underground. And I-- we each pretended we had a telephone. And my mother heard Uta, hier Evy Go-- I wasn't supposed to say my name.
